title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary:
The reviewer wants four changes to the OpenBao bootstrap scripts:

1. **Replace `base64 -w 0` with `base64 | tr -d '\n'`** in `init-unseal.sh` for macOS compatibility, since `-w 0` is GNU-only.
2. **Replace `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=true` with `VAULT_CACERT=/openbao/tls/ca.crt`** in `common.sh` (and related exec wrappers) to use proper TLS certificate verification instead of disabling it entirely.
3. **Add inline comments (or defer role creation)** in `setup-auth.sh` to clarify that non-management Kubernetes auth mounts intentionally lack config and will fail until those clusters are provisioned.
4. **Sanitize `kv_path`** in `write-bootstrap-secrets.sh` to prevent shell injection from single quotes in path values, either by escaping or passing via environment variable.

code_comments.body:
**`base64 -w 0` is GNU coreutilsâ€“only and fails on macOS**

`base64 -w 0` (disable line wrapping) is a GNU coreutils flag. On macOS/BSD the `base64` binary does not recognise `-w` and will exit with an error:

```
base64: illegal option -- w
```

This means any operator running `init-unseal.sh` from a macOS workstation (a common dev setup) will fail to store the unseal keys/root token â€” the most critical step of the entire bootstrap.

Because the Secret is created with `kubectl apply -f -`, a multi-line base64 value would still be valid YAML (the value is on one line in the `data:` map). For cross-platform compatibility, replace the GNU-specific flag with `tr -d '\n'` after `base64`, which works on both Linux and macOS:

```suggestion
  encoded=$(echo -n "${init_output}" | base64 | tr -d '\n')
```

Line: 89

Comment:

`base64 -w 0` (disable line wrapping) is a GNU coreutils flag. On macOS/BSD the `base64` binary does not recognise `-w` and will exit with an error:

```

```

This means any operator running `init-unseal.sh` from a macOS workstation (a common dev setup) will fail to store the unseal keys/root token â€” the most critical step of the entire bootstrap.

Because the Secret is created with `kubectl apply -f -`, a multi-line base64 value would still be valid YAML (the value is on one line in the `data:` map). For cross-platform compatibility, replace the GNU-specific flag with `tr -d '\n'` after `base64`, which works on both Linux and macOS:

```suggestion
  encoded=$(echo -n "${init_output}" | base64 | tr -d '\n')
```

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Replaced `base64 -w 0` with `base64 | tr -d '\n'` at init-unseal.sh:91 for cross-platform compatibility (works on both GNU/Linux and macOS/BSD).

code_comments.body:
**`VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=true` disables TLS verification â€” use `VAULT_CACERT` instead**

Setting `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=true` disables certificate verification entirely for all `bao` commands in every bootstrap script. While TLS is still used for transport encryption, the server's identity is never checked â€” a man-in-the-middle with a different certificate would succeed silently.

The self-signed CA cert is already available inside the pod at `/openbao/tls/ca.crt` (mounted from the same `openbao-tls` Secret). Replacing skip-verify with an explicit CA reference gives actual TLS authentication at zero cost:

```suggestion
export VAULT_CACERT="${VAULT_CACERT:-/openbao/tls/ca.crt}"
```

You would also need to remove the explicit `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY` injection from the `env` wrappers in `bao_exec` / `bao_exec_stdin` and replace it with `VAULT_CACERT` â€” but the change in `common.sh` together with the `export` here means the environment variable will already be present in the exec env when forwarded via `kubectl exec`'s inherited environment (or explicitly via `env VAULT_CACERT=...`).

This also affects the equivalent `kube_exec` / `kube_exec_stdin` wrappers in `init-unseal.sh` which carry the same `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY` injection.

The self-signed CA cert is already available inside the pod at `/openbao/tls/ca.crt` (mounted from the same `openbao-tls` Secret). Replacing skip-verify with an explicit CA reference gives actual TLS authentication at zero cost:

```suggestion

```

You would also need to remove the explicit `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY` injection from the `env` wrappers in `bao_exec` / `bao_exec_stdin` and replace it with `VAULT_CACERT` â€” but the change in `common.sh` together with the `export` here means the environment variable will already be present in the exec env when forwarded via `kubectl exec`'s inherited environment (or explicitly via `env VAULT_CACERT=...`).

This also affects the equivalent `kube_exec` / `kube_exec_stdin` wrappers in `init-unseal.sh` which carry the same `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY` injection.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````

code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Replaced `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=true` with `VAULT_CACERT=/openbao/tls/ca.crt` in common.sh:19. Also updated all exec wrappers: `bao_exec` (common.sh:38), `bao_exec_stdin` (common.sh:48), and the equivalent wrappers in init-unseal.sh (lines 34, 43) and write-bootstrap-secrets.sh (line 67) to pass `VAULT_CACERT` instead of `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY`.

code_comments.body:
**Non-management Kubernetes auth mounts have roles but no config â€” authentication will silently fail until configured**

The script enables and creates ESO roles for `kubernetes/control-plane`, `kubernetes/hypervisor`, and `kubernetes/storage` but never calls `bao write auth/kubernetes/<cluster>/config`. Without a config write, OpenBao has no way to validate service account tokens from those clusters â€” every authentication attempt by ESO on those clusters will fail with a 403 from the Kubernetes TokenReview API (or an OpenBao internal error if it falls back to auto-discovery and can't reach that cluster's API server).

The documentation notes this is intentional ("deferred until those clusters are provisioned"), but the current code creates roles that cannot be used, which can cause confusing errors when those clusters come online later.

Consider adding a comment directly in the script to make this explicit for operators, and consider whether an explicit guard (e.g., checking for a `CONFIGURE_MANAGEMENT_ONLY=true` env var) would make the deferred-configuration intent clearer than prose in the docs. Alternatively, deferring even the role creation until the cluster is configured would prevent a partial-but-unusable auth mount state.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown

Line: 49-57

Comment:
**Non-management Kubernetes auth mounts have roles but no config â€” authentication will silently fail until configured**

The script enables and creates ESO roles for `kubernetes/control-plane`, `kubernetes/hypervisor`, and `kubernetes/storage` but never calls `bao write auth/kubernetes/<cluster>/config`. Without a config write, OpenBao has no way to validate service account tokens from those clusters â€” every authentication attempt by ESO on those clusters will fail with a 403 from the Kubernetes TokenReview API (or an OpenBao internal error if it falls back to auto-discovery and can't reach that cluster's API server).

The documentation notes this is intentional ("deferred until those clusters are provisioned"), but the current code creates roles that cannot be used, which can cause confusing errors when those clusters come online later.

Consider adding a comment directly in the script to make this explicit for operators, and consider whether an explicit guard (e.g., checking for a `CONFIGURE_MANAGEMENT_ONLY=true` env var) would make the deferred-configuration intent clearer than prose in the docs. Alternatively, deferring even the role creation until the cluster is configured would prevent a partial-but-unusable auth mount state.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Added a 10-line NOTE(CC-0009) comment block at setup-auth.sh:73-82 explaining that non-management cluster auth config is intentionally deferred until those clusters are provisioned, and that pre-creating roles avoids a second bootstrap pass â€” only `bao write auth/kubernetes/<cluster>/config` is needed later.

code_comments.body:
**`kv_path` is single-quoted but not sanitized â€” single quotes in path values would break the `sh -c` command**

`kv_path` is embedded in the inner shell command as a single-quoted string:

```bash
sh -c "bao kv put '${kv_path}' ${put_args}"
```

If a future caller passes a `kv_path` containing a single quote (e.g., `kv-v2/openstack/it's-a-path`), the shell command will be misparsed and `bao kv put` will fail with a syntax error â€” or worse, the single quote could terminate the single-quoted segment early and allow unintended shell token injection.

The current callers use safe paths (`kv-v2/bootstrap/keystone-admin`, etc.), so there is no immediate bug, but hardening this for future callers would be safer. A minimal fix is to printf-escape the path:

```bash
local safe_path
safe_path=$(printf '%s' "${kv_path}" | sed "s/'/'\\\\''/g")
# ...
sh -c "bao kv put '${safe_path}' ${put_args}"
```

Or, if `bao` supports reading from environment variables, pass the path via an env var to the inner shell and reference it as `$PATH_VAR` instead of interpolating it literally.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: deploy/openbao/bootstrap/write-bootstrap-secrets.sh
Line: 59-63

Comment:

`kv_path` is embedded in the inner shell command as a single-quoted string:

```bash
sh -c "bao kv put '${kv_path}' ${put_args}"
```

If a future caller passes a `kv_path` containing a single quote (e.g., `kv-v2/openstack/it's-a-path`), the shell command will be misparsed and `bao kv put` will fail with a syntax error â€” or worse, the single quote could terminate the single-quoted segment early and allow unintended shell token injection.

The current callers use safe paths (`kv-v2/bootstrap/keystone-admin`, etc.), so there is no immediate bug, but hardening this for future callers would be safer. A minimal fix is to printf-escape the path:

```bash
local safe_path
safe_path=$(printf '%s' "${kv_path}" | sed "s/'/'\\\\''/g")
# ...
sh -c "bao kv put '${safe_path}' ${put_args}"
```

Or, if `bao` supports reading from environment variables, pass the path via an env var to the inner shell and reference it as `$PATH_VAR` instead of interpolating it literally.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Refactored write-bootstrap-secrets.sh to pass kv_path as an environment variable (BAO_KV_PATH) to the inner shell at lines 63-69 instead of interpolating it into the `sh -c` command string. This prevents shell injection if a future caller passes a path containing single quotes or metacharacters.

resolution_summary: All four review comments were addressed. Comment [0] fixed cross-platform base64 encoding by replacing `base64 -w 0` with `base64 | tr -d '\n'`. Comment [1] replaced `VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY=true` with `VAULT_CACERT` pointing to the self-signed CA cert across all exec wrappers in common.sh, init-unseal.sh, and write-bootstrap-secrets.sh. Comment [2] added a detailed NOTE comment in setup-auth.sh explaining the deferred auth config for non-management clusters. Comment [3] refactored write-bootstrap-secrets.sh to pass kv_path via the BAO_KV_PATH environment variable instead of interpolating it into the sh -c command string, preventing shell injection.
